result:
The first thing that arrives is the smell of bread drifting from a basement vent on the north side of the city, somewhere between the river and the elevated train. It is 3:17 a.m., and the streetlamps have just flickered their small, conspiratorial twilight that means the grid is conserving power again. I am walking because the buses stopped running and because my apartment tastes like overdue rent and the quiet accusation of dishes left in the sink since Tuesday. The smell of yeast and toasted crust is a document written in a language I almost remember from childhood, when Saturday mornings meant my grandmother tearing hot chunks from a round loaf, blowing on her fingertips, scolding the steam for being too eager. I do not consciously decide to follow the scent, yet my shoes cross against the don’t-walk sign the way tongues seek the tender spot where a tooth was extracted—curiosity masquerading as ache.

Behind the vent’s grille, a man in a white T-shirt is pulling linen-lined baskets from an oven the size of a Buick. His forearms are mapped with floury continents. He looks up, sees my face pressed ghostlike to the slot, and does what strangers rarely do at 3:17: he smiles with his entire mouth, not merely the civilized corners, and lifts a loaf toward me as if offering a newborn to a distant relative. I shake my head—no money, no language to explain that gluten and I signed a non-aggression pact years ago—but he tears the loaf in half anyway, stretches to the vent, and pushes the still-porous heart of it through the slats. Steam webs upward; the crust crackles like a log collapsing in a campfire. I accept. The first tear burns my fingers; the second dissolves on my tongue and tastes like every safe room I have ever wanted. We stand there, wordless, citizen and baker, separated by iron mesh and a conspiracy of carbohydrates. When I finally walk on, the loaf is dwindling in my hands like a lit fuse, and I understand I have been given not food but evidence: that mercy is portable, that it can fit through a grate.

At the bridge the river is practicing its ancient mirrors. Neon blurs on the black water—bar signs, traffic arrows, a billboard selling vacation condos where each balcony comes with “a breeze calibrated for contentment.” I tear another piece of bread, wondering if somewhere downstream the river will carry microscopic crumbs into the intake pipe of that very complex, so that future residents drinking their curated breeze will taste this anonymous moment. The city is full of such subterranean correspondence: gum pressed under theater seats, love letters slipped into library books, the faint click when a stranger’s phone photo accidentally captures your shoulder in perpetuity. We live atop layers of unnoticed donations. I once read that the weight of London’s sidewalks increases by several tons each year just from the dust of decomposed skin—every citizen an unwitting benefactor of themselves.

A cyclist without headlights glides past, earbuds glowing like coals. The tiny thump of his playlist leaks into the night—an eight-bit snippet of the Super Mario theme—and suddenly I am eight years old on the carpet of a friend’s den, elbows raw from carpet burn, trying to jump the lava chasm on level 2-4. Time is less a river than a loom: the same shuttle returns carrying different colored threads. My mother is alive in that den, wearing jeans that taper at the ankle; my father still smokes Merit Ultra Lights and keeps his disappointment in a tackle box beneath the bar. I swallow the memory with the last of the bread, surprised by how bland nostalgia tastes without butter.

On the far side of the bridge I discover a small park shaped like a postage stamp, bordered on three sides by condos whose windows pulse bluish from televisions left on to deter burglars. In the center stands a single tree lit from below by a spotlight that has no off switch. The tree is a ginkgo, its leaves prehistoric fans, and beneath it a woman in a silver parka is organizing fallen foliage into a perfect circle, color side up. As I watch, she plucks a leaf, writes on it with a marker, and places it back gold side down. I ask, from the path, what she is cataloging. “Apologies,” she says without looking up. “One per leaf. When the wind takes them, the apology is considered delivered.” She offers the marker. I hesitate, then write on a leaf: I’m sorry I keep pretending I know where I’m going. I lay it among its siblings. We work in silence, manufacturing absolution for debts we can’t name. After a while she zips her parka, nods goodbye, and leaves the circle intact. I sit on a bench. Minutes or centuries later a breeze stirs; leaves lift, scatter. Somewhere a dog barks once, embarrassed by its own urgency. I feel lighter, though I can’t decide if it’s forgiveness or simply having written the sentence down.

The sky begins its slow bleach toward dawn. I remember that today is the anniversary of a failed friendship, a person I loved like a second alphabet but who moved away and stopped replying, the way astronauts lose radio contact behind the moon. I decide to send them nothing—no text, no emoji, no late-night voice memo—because absence too can be a form of correspondence, a negative space that outlines what still matters. Instead I open my phone’s map and drop a pin in the middle of the river, labeling it “unsent.” Somewhere, servers store that secret coordinate forever, a shrine no one will pilgrimage to.

I walk home past the bakery again. The vent is quiet now, the ovens cooling, but the smell lingers like a rumor. On the doorstep of my building lies a single ginkgo leaf, gold side up. No wind could have carried it this far. I bring it inside, slip it between pages of a book I keep promising to finish. Years from now I will rediscover it, flattened and brittle, and wonder which of us preserved the other.

Inside, the dishes are still waiting, but they look less accusatory, more like students who stayed after class hoping for extra credit. I fill the sink, add soap, watch the bubbles rise—small universes with brief, shimmering constants. Somewhere in the foam I think I see the reflection of the baker’s arms, the cyclist’s earbuds, the apology circle, the river’s neon script. All of it stays long enough to be observed, then pops, releasing the faintest scent of fresh bread into the kitchen, where it clings to the curtains like a treaty.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
